speaker: interviewer
text: --[INAUDIBLE] with Robert Ness on October the 31st, 1995 in Skokie, Illinois. The subject, songs of the ghettos in the camps. From Estonia, you were transported to concentration camp Stutthof.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Stutthof, you had occasion to sing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Now, but you see, you have to get a picture. It would be better. You know, Stutthof, suddenly, it's a big camp, huge camp, 100,000 inmates. My gosh. And suddenly, it's not only Jews. Oh, now, it was other nationalities. Oh, it was like security. Because you're not only Jews. Look at it. They have Poles, and the-- and Frenchmen, and-- and the Russians, and-- and Ukrainian, and Lithuanians-- wow-- I mean, Danes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in one sense, people felt a little bit that it's an advancement from the place in Estonia, where it was just Jewish camp that you couldn't tell. One of the days it's going to be all killed, or shot, or whatever it is. So you have to keep that in mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, we arrived besides the trip on the boat for three days. And they gave us some food. And people ate it, whatever was given in the first day, I among them. So I was so hungry as can be. And I was going through my pockets every time thinking there is some food. I'll find something. There was nothing found. There was nothing there. So, and then we marched from Danzig at that time, which was Gdansk now, to the camps. Stutthof-- Stutthof, I think, was on the land, what used to be Freie Stadt Danzig before the war, the corridor, the-- this free city of Danzig. And we came to Stutthof. And there was somebody that I mentioned to you, this fellow, Zukowski who came out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he started to ask, is there some-- some news Nussbaum from Raczki. So you have a news [? bum ?] in the crowd. And somebody said yes, yes. He says, the old one or the young one?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Polish we were talking. And then he says, the young one. And lo and behold, here is our neighbor from Raczki before the war-- knew my father, knew me. He was a leader-- a leader. He was a member of the [NON-ENGLISH] they used to call it in the-- in before the war. [NON-ENGLISH] POW. It sounds like a prisoner of war. But the POW in Polish before the war was the-- before the First World War or during the First World War were the ones with [INAUDIBLE] that were in his organization and liberated Poland and became-- Poland became independent.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had a concession on tobacco and liquor, which was quite a big concession he was. But he was our neighbor. And he was dealing with my father as-- as businessman.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we used to bring him-- for Passover, we used to bring him matzos and wine, the standard. We used to give him-- that we used to make ourselves, the wine, a very decent man and a nice man. And here, suddenly, he was like an angel to me. So I-- he starting to ask where is father, mother. I told him what I knew. And I told him that mother was killed and Pola was killed. And so he was the one who kind of indicated that, you know, we have a young priest from Raczki, Naroszewicz. And my sister was-- knew him going to school before the war. And he knew the family. And he says, I'll talk to him. And I'll be a son of a gun. Here, the next day we come to the disinfection, and by gosh, Nussbaum, call me out. And sure enough Naroszewicz, that young priest, arrives.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's talked. Oh, I was already looking that he just comes in already with some friends here. You know? You're already a big shot, so to speak. Big shot, I mean in the sense that somebody knows you at least. He's not to complete nobody, you know, a number. And so when we came to the camp, the question arose about work or something. So Zukowski kind of said, you know what? Let me talk to Naroszewicz again. Maybe he can help you and get-- he knows the block of [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewee
text: When you know the people in the power in the structure, it makes a hell of a difference. That was a big break. He says, maybe he will talk to him. And there are six priests. And maybe he can find some work for you there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, sure. And I talked to him. And he said, let me see what I can do. And I'll be a son of a gun. The next week, sure enough, I got my job as an-- what would you call it? As an-- An orderly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Orderly. Absolutely, an orderly. And then there was an old Kashub. But now, this is already I am from the Jewish quarter, which is block 13. And I'm going to block [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 9, I think. It's a 9 or 6, doesn't really matter. OK. It was in block 6 or 9 they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: The priests?

speaker: interviewee
text: The priests, right. And suddenly, among the priests, I started to kind of cleaning after him, too, and helping there. And as matter of fact, there is a book by one of the priests who was in that camp. And he mentions-- my sister got it from another one in connection with the-- with her work she was doing with the Holocaust Foundation. And he-- she brought the book. And I telling-- and we're sitting at the table. I say, oh, my gosh. There was a priest, [INAUDIBLE] who used to talk about the Poles were Polenizing. And the German were Germanizing them. And he knew Danzig was the capital of the Kashubs. So I said, he used to buttonhole whoever he could. And I thought, I'll be a son of a gun. I go through the book. And I take a look at the index. And sure enough, right there, that particular priest is mentioned how he used to buttonhole people and when anybody wants to listen about the history of the Kashubs. It was a fishery type of minority. And they're really Poles. But anyway, that what he was talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in that part, he says about the boy, a Jewish boy who used to sing song for them. He said, the date, well, it wasn't 18 or 19. It was Bob Ness. And I wasn't any 16, 18. I was 15 years old. So, but it's nothing to do with camp. The later songs that I sang for them was a Sunday. The people were on that work. They liked the songs. Because I used to-- my voice was good. So I used to sing for them. Would you sing a few of the songs that you sang for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the songs-- believe it. Oh, that song was a Yiddish song, which is really from the Soviet Russian part about Birobidzhan. I mean, now, that one I learned in school in 1940, '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: You follow me? That was before the Germans entered Slonim. They entered Slonim in June 25, 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's a story about fishermen going through for the fishing with nets, with-- and it's in Yiddish. And it's about Birobidzhan. They made it-- I-- now, the Russian song is [NON-ENGLISH] under fishing as a-- I don't even know whether the Russian part has anything about those other nationalities, and Chinese, and yellow, [INAUDIBLE] Jews, and so forth. But be that as it may, the song I knew in Yiddish I never knew at that time, the Russian part of it. The song I knew was in Yiddish. And it goes something like that. [SINGING IN YIDDISH] That's all I remember. So I sang that kind of songs to them. And there was some other songs.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a song, 'Lakes of Masur.'

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, see, that is a folk song, a Polish folk songs that the Masur around the-- the-- the places where I come from, it's considered the Masur area, the-- the lakes of Masuria. And there is the dialect. The dialect was such that they wouldn't say a sh. They said it is a se. They didn't use that word sh. And the story is about this [INAUDIBLE] that he died. And he's already there. And he's laid out. But, well, if somebody would sing, if somebody would sing to him, he might wake up. Because the soul of the Masurs is such that you-- when you sing, then it moves. And that's how I remember this when I'm [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How does it go?

speaker: interviewee
text: It goes like--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is Polish. [SINGING IN POLISH] This is a very common Polish song. But I-- and I learned a little bit more, the details. There were some Polish boys from our-- from Raczki where I came from. By way of Zukowski, I met some of them. But not only that. An interesting part that I found out later, that my father after the war when he was looking for, where I am, that he heard something that I might have been surviving, that I survived, I was at the camp. So he was in the [NON-ENGLISH] marketplace. And he met one of the men that he knew from before the war. And he says to him-- [NON-ENGLISH] Nussbaum. [NON-ENGLISH] Mr. Nussbaum, your son is living. My boy was with him. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] My son was with him in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, how would he have known he was from this crowd, the locality, that Naroszewicz introduced me to-- to boys from Raczki? So my father found out, so he knew. He was sure that I was in Stutthof, at least I'm out already from Estonia. And I'm in Stutthof. Do you remember any other songs that you sang to the priests in Stutthof? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't be surprised that I might have sang [NON-ENGLISH]. Because it was kind of a live one. It has a number of melodies, the one with the train, [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I might have sang [NON-ENGLISH]. But if I was singing [NON-ENGLISH], it wouldn't have been the one that people know. that-- about the girl that is standing. My version was a more children-like that I remember, which goes, what is taller than a house? And what is swifter than a mouse? And the chimney is taller than a house. And a cat is swifter than a mouse. And what is-- what is-- which water is without sand? And what king is without land? And it goes, the tears, the water from tears is without sand. And the-- a king of cards is without land. And then it goes, which water is without a fish? And what house is without a [NON-ENGLISH], without a table? So you see, I might have been singing that part of it. How did that go in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish, it goes, [SINGING IN YIDDISH] And then it goes [NON-ENGLISH]. Then this other one is-- [SINGING IN YIDDISH] I know they like that. It was-- so in-- that's about it, I think. There were some other songs I might have sang. But anyway, I did perform on Sundays. And sometime I used to get some cookies. And what-- what used to be-- there was the Kashubs, you see. Here, I come to a camp. It's not the Jews. They get a-- they get packages. You know, and letters are being allowed to be written. Well, so suddenly, I became again my own translation business. In a sense, it used to come-- it had to be in German written. I happened to know German pretty well.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- we used to write cards for them in German, like saying hello to the family and so on. And I am well. Or, can you send us something? Whatever it was I used to do that I used to get a piece of bread. I used to get some-- sometimes even a piece of meat, a meat-- a meat of-- what do you call it-- bacon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or-- but then keep in mind, I came already the late part after the summer of '44 and after the uprising in Warsaw, August. Forget it. The front was getting closer. Now, Zukowski didn't get packages anymore. He used to get packages from home, as many of the other Polish inmates used to get. The Danes, I think I mentioned it to you in the other part, used to get from the Red Cross. Oh, it was chocolate, with the sardines. I mean, whoever heard of such a thing. And they used to give it to some smaller Jewish boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thinking back, kind of summarizing on the subject of songs, the songs that you learned, that you sang, and that you remember, what-- what added dimension?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there's no question about it. I know in my case it was-- it was a nourishment, you know, to see the more bigger picture, to see it more as to the sufferings, as to the-- you're making jokes out of it about like, the air of the camp, like [NON-ENGLISH]. Like that one, [? Susie. ?] I mean, it was what you would call-- the German call it [NON-ENGLISH] humor, the humor of the gallows. You know, it was there. It's-- it's-- To give you an idea, the humor of the gallows is- is this joke that [INAUDIBLE] used to like to tell. And he was the one who told it to [? Frankfurter. ?] [? Frankfurter ?] has it in his book, Frankfurter [INAUDIBLE]. It's a beautiful joke. And this particular Jewish fellow, he runs away from Poland. And he-- he comes to Portugal, 1942. Oh, well, he's already out from Hitler and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he comes to the-- to the American consulate to get a visa to America. And the console says to him-- he says, no, no, no. I say, you're born in Poland? He said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said well, you know, the Polish visa, the Polish quota-- at that time they had quotas-- the Polish quota is filled for about eight years. So he said, no, no. Come eight years from now. And he says, we'll see whether we can-- and the guy already walks out kind of depressed. And he's just ready to go out from the garden. And then he turns around.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, Mr. Console, you told me I should come back eight years from the when the Polish quota filled. He says, that's right. That's right. You told me to come back eight years from now? He says, that's right. Should I come in the morning or the afternoon? You know, so this is kind of the humor of the gallows. You know? So it gives you a lively-- its'-- it's-- it's-- So there was-- it was spiritual nourishment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there's no question about it. It's no question about it. You know, when you could-- you could-- I know I could see-- now, I liked it. And I enjoyed the song and so on. But I could see it when I used to sing for the others in the camp. People were listening.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know, I just went-- yeah, I heard. You know this one? Do you know? Did you hear of that one, you know? It was just so obviously just no question that it wasn't just merely the treachery was such and that so and that everything was broken, and broken, and broken.

speaker: interviewee
text: If there was a little bit of not even freedom, but a little bit of not so much oppression, that if you didn't work 28 hours, 24 hours, and they didn't grind you to the point of destruction, [BACKGROUND NOISE] there was always this development of some songs and so on. And that's all that I remember. You know, I went through-- I was in Slonim, Grodno, in Vilna, and then-- in Estonia. And I learned songs in every one of them. Not so much perhaps from Slonim. But-- but there must have been there some, too, that I knew. I think that the Russian one I told you came from there. A thought about the policeman was hilarious, about the cemetery stuff. I remember that. I always used to laugh about it. And so people who I used to sing used to laugh about it. Because obviously, it's humorous.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's Kafkaesque, as I mentioned to you, you know, to have-- to have this kind of policeman watching a cemetery. What is he watching? What is he protecting there? Ah, it's protecting the diseased from the young people who don't give them rest. Where would you get this? It's-- it's-- it's quite an imagination, you know? Well, thank you very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for sharing the songs that you remember. And it was a pleasure listening to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have a good voice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Thank you.
